metadata.title: Standard Internships Put You Near the Work. Co-op Puts You In the System.

beats.narration_text: Olá — this is Liam, in for Bear. Based on the essay 'Standard Internships Put You Near the Work. Co-op Puts You In the System.' by Aditi Shinde, for NortheasternISE / Humanitarians AI. What's the difference between an internship and a co-op? Not time. Not pay. The difference is ontological. An internship places you adjacent to the system. A co-op places you inside it — with real stakes, real accountability, and the city itself as a living case study.

props.segment: Standard Internships Put You Near the Work. Co-op Puts You In the System.
props.command: What's the difference between an internship and a co-op?
output: Internship: adjacency — you observe the system from outside.
Co-op: containment — you are accountable to the system.

beats.narration_text: Month zero: two students, same first day. One gets an internship, one gets a co-op. At month three, the intern is shadowing project reviews and learning the vocabulary. The co-op student has been in the stand-up since week one. They filed the tickets. They wrote the code. They own a piece of the system. At month six, the intern has a great reference and a polished portfolio project. The co-op student found the bug that delayed patient discharge. That is not a metaphor — that is the specific claim of this kind of experiential education.

animated_deck.description: Split timeline: two student trajectories from month 0 to month 6. Left: intern — observatory, shadowing, portfolio. Right: co-op — stand-ups, tickets, ownership. Both tracks converge at month 0, diverge immediately.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Month zero: two students, same first day. One gets an internship, one gets a co-op. At month three, the intern is shadowing project reviews and learning the voc

beats.narration_text: Here is the geometry. Adjacency: the student is a circle partially overlapping the system circle. They touch it. They influence it slightly. They are not inside it. Containment: the student circle is fully nested inside the system circle. The system's boundary is now the student's boundary. When the system fails, the student is accountable. When the system succeeds, the student can honestly claim a share. Two words — adjacent versus contained — encode completely different learning conditions.

manim.description: Side-by-side Manim diagram. Left: two circles, partial overlap, labeled 'intern' and 'system'. Right: small circle fully inside large circle, labeled 'co-op student' inside 'system'. Balance scale appears and tilts toward containment side.

beats.narration_text: The city is the curriculum. That is not marketing copy — it is a pedagogical claim. The co-op student is placed in a real organization in a real city, working on real problems with real consequences. The patient whose discharge is delayed is a real patient. The system they are debugging is in production. The city's hospitals, firms, and labs are classrooms without walls. The measure of the education is not a grade — it is whether the work persisted after the student left.

artifactLines: Real organization. Real system. Real consequences.
The patient whose discharge is delayed is a real patient.
The measure: did the work persist after the student left?
The city's institutions are classrooms without walls.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Internships and co-ops differ ontologically, not just in duration or pay. Adjacency means you observe the system; containment means you are accountable to it. At two years out, only the co-op student can say 'I found the bug that delayed patient discharge' — not as a project they were assigned, but as a failure they owned inside a live system. Same credential on a resume. Different competence underneath it.

props.artifactTitle: Standard Internships Put You Near the Work. Co-op Puts You In the System.
artifactLines: Internships and co-ops differ ontologically, not just temporally.
Adjacency: you observe the system. Containment: you are accountable to it.
At 2 years: only the co-op student can claim the bug they fixed in production.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt — read it with me. 'Design the ideal co-op experience for a role that doesn't currently offer co-ops. What does inside the system look like for a data scientist at a hospital?' That phrase — inside the system — is the design constraint. You can't just describe tasks. You have to describe accountability. What breaks if the co-op student gets it wrong? What persists after they leave? Run this in your own Claude session. Liam, in for Bear.

props.segment: Standard Internships Put You Near the Work. Co-op Puts You In the System.
props.command: Design the ideal co-op experience for a role that doesn't currently offer co-ops. What does 'inside the system' look like for a data scientist at a hospital?
